accepted_findings.source_text: Any amounts not paid when due will accrue interest at the rate of one and one-half percent (1.5%) per month
accepted_findings.proposed_text: Any amounts not paid when due will accrue interest at the rate of one percent (1.0%) per month
accepted_findings.external_comment: We'd like to reduce the late-payment interest rate to 1% per month, which aligns with market practice for buyer-side SaaS agreements of this scale.
accepted_findings.materiality_rationale: 1.5% per month (18% APR) is above typical buyer-side market rates; Profile specifies 1% per month maximum as our standard position on late fees.
accepted_findings.position: 1% per month maximum.
accepted_findings.jurisdiction_assumed: Delaware; usury and late-fee caps vary by state but 1.5%/month is legally permissible in most U.S. jurisdictions including Delaware; issue is commercial reasonableness not legal compliance
accepted_findings.suggested_text: Any amounts not paid when due will accrue interest at the rate of one percent (1.0%) per month
accepted_findings._rationale_explanation: Pure playbook string-matching: contract says 1.5%, standard says 1%, change to 1%—no engagement with deal context, leverage, or whether pushing on this minor issue is worth it given the high-leverage posture.

accepted_findings.proposed_text: 3.6 Disputed Amounts. Customer may withhold payment of any specific disputed amount on an invoice, provided that Customer notifies Lattice in writing of the nature and basis of the dispute within fifteen (15) days of receipt of the invoice. Undisputed amounts shall remain due on the standard payment terms set forth in Section 3.2. The Parties will work in good faith to resolve any such dispute promptly. Amounts ultimately determined to be owed by Customer will be paid within ten (10) days of resolution, without late fees or interest for the period during which the amount was in good-faith dispute.
accepted_findings.external_comment: We'd like to add a provision allowing Customer to withhold payment on specific disputed invoice amounts while undisputed portions remain payable on standard terms. This is standard in SaaS agreements and protects both parties by scoping the withholding right and providing a resolution framework.
accepted_findings.materiality_rationale: Without a dispute-withholding mechanism, Customer must either pay invoices in full (even when billing errors or contractual disagreements exist) or breach the payment covenant by non-payment. This creates a binary choice that harms Customer when good-faith disputes arise over usage calculations, rate application, or scope.
accepted_findings.position: Customer may withhold specific disputed amounts; notice within 15 days; undisputed amounts remain payable; no late fees during good-faith dispute period.
accepted_findings.fallback: Customer may withhold disputed amounts; notice within 10 days; interest accrues during dispute but is waived if Customer prevails.
accepted_findings.jurisdiction_assumed: Delaware; dispute-withholding provisions are commercial risk-allocation and not governed by specific statutory regimes; Delaware contract law permits such provisions
accepted_findings.suggested_text: 3.6 Disputed Amounts. Customer may withhold payment of any specific disputed amount on an invoice, provided that Customer notifies Lattice in writing of the nature and basis of the dispute within fifteen (15) days of receipt of the invoice. Undisputed amounts shall remain due on the standard payment terms set forth in Section 3.2. The Parties will work in good faith to resolve any such dispute promptly. Amounts ultimately determined to be owed by Customer will be paid within ten (10) days of resolution, without late fees or interest for the period during which the amount was in good-faith dispute.
accepted_findings._rationale_explanation: Articulates standard risk of binary pay-or-breach dilemma in any customer contract, but doesn't engage with this specific deal's leverage position, contract structure, dollar values, or whether the existing 15-day notice provision is sufficient given the deal context.

accepted_findings.source_text: Lattice may increase the Fees applicable to a Renewal Term by providing Customer with written notice (which may be by email) at least sixty (60) days prior to the end of the then-current Subscription Term. Any such increase will take effect at the start of the next Renewal Term.
accepted_findings.proposed_text: Lattice may increase the Fees applicable to a Renewal Term by up to five percent (5%) annually, by providing Customer with written notice (which may be by email) at least sixty (60) days prior to the end of the then-current Subscription Term. Any increase exceeding five percent (5%) in a given year will be subject to mutual agreement. Any such increase will take effect at the start of the next Renewal Term.
accepted_findings.external_comment: We'd like to cap annual fee increases at 5% per year, with larger increases subject to mutual agreement. Uncapped escalation creates unpredictable cost exposure on an evergreen agreement, particularly when combined with automatic renewal.
accepted_findings.materiality_rationale: Unlimited unilateral price increases expose Customer to unbounded cost escalation on a contract that auto-renews with 60-day notice; Lattice can increase Fees by any amount year-over-year, and Customer's only remedy is non-renewal (which requires 60 days' advance planning). On a critical-path SaaS service, this creates pricing leverage that compounds annually.
accepted_findings.position: Cap at 5% annually; increases above 5% require mutual agreement.
accepted_findings.fallback: Cap at 7% annually, or increases tied to CPI-U with a floor of 3% and ceiling of 8%.
accepted_findings.jurisdiction_assumed: Delaware; price-escalation caps are standard commercial provisions and not subject to specific statutory interpretation
accepted_findings.suggested_text: Lattice may increase the Fees applicable to a Renewal Term by up to five percent (5%) annually, by providing Customer with written notice (which may be by email) at least sixty (60) days prior to the end of the then-current Subscription Term. Any increase exceeding five percent (5%) in a given year will be subject to mutual agreement. Any such increase will take effect at the start of the next Renewal Term.
accepted_findings._rationale_explanation: Engages with the specific contract structure (auto-renewal mechanism, 60-day notice interplay, and unilateral increase power) and articulates how these provisions compound to create leverage, though stops short of weighing client's specific deal posture or recommending action given their high leverage position.

accepted_findings.source_text: If any amount owing by Customer is more than thirty (30) days overdue, Lattice may, without limiting its other rights and remedies, suspend Customer's access to the Subscription Services until such amounts are paid in full. Lattice will provide Customer with at least ten (10) days' prior notice that Customer's account is overdue before suspending the Subscription Services.
accepted_findings.proposed_text: If any undisputed amount owing by Customer is more than thirty (30) days overdue, Lattice may, without limiting its other rights and remedies, suspend Customer's access to the Subscription Services until such amounts are paid in full. Lattice will provide Customer with at least ten (10) days' prior notice that Customer's account is overdue before suspending the Subscription Services. Lattice may not suspend the Subscription Services for non-payment of any amount that Customer has disputed in good faith in accordance with Section 3.6.
accepted_findings.external_comment: We'd like to clarify that suspension for non-payment applies only to undisputed amounts, and cannot be triggered by good-faith disputed amounts. This aligns the suspension right with the dispute-resolution framework and prevents suspension leverage in contractual disagreements.
accepted_findings.materiality_rationale: As drafted, Lattice may suspend for any overdue amount, including amounts Customer disputes in good faith. This creates suspension leverage unrelated to actual payment default, particularly if the proposed dispute-withholding provision (Finding 003) is added. Limiting suspension to undisputed amounts prevents weaponization of the suspension right during invoice disputes.
accepted_findings.position: Suspension applies only to undisputed amounts; no suspension during good-faith dispute resolution.
accepted_findings.jurisdiction_assumed: Delaware; suspension-right scoping is a commercial-terms issue without specific statutory governance
accepted_findings.suggested_text: If any undisputed amount owing by Customer is more than thirty (30) days overdue, Lattice may, without limiting its other rights and remedies, suspend Customer's access to the Subscription Services until such amounts are paid in full. Lattice will provide Customer with at least ten (10) days' prior notice that Customer's account is overdue before suspending the Subscription Services. Lattice may not suspend the Subscription Services for non-payment of any amount that Customer has disputed in good faith in accordance with Section 3.6.
accepted_findings._rationale_explanation: Engages with the specific contract structure by cross-referencing Finding 003's dispute-withholding provision and explaining how the two provisions interact to create leverage risk in this deal.

accepted_findings.source_text: if Customer terminates this Agreement other than for Lattice's uncured material breach pursuant to Section 4.3, Customer shall pay all Fees that would have been payable for the remainder of the then-current Subscription Term
accepted_findings.proposed_text: if Customer terminates this Agreement other than for Lattice's uncured material breach pursuant to Section 4.3, Customer shall pay, as liquidated damages and not as a penalty, fifty percent (50%) of the Fees that would have been payable for the remainder of the then-current Subscription Term
accepted_findings.external_comment: We'd like to revise the early-termination payment to 50% of remaining Fees, structured as liquidated damages. Full payment of remaining Fees eliminates Customer's ability to exit for convenience and creates a lock-in dynamic. A 50% payment reasonably compensates Lattice for lost margin while preserving Customer's exit right.
accepted_findings.materiality_rationale: Full payment of remaining Fees on early termination converts this into a non-cancelable obligation with no practical exit right; Customer pays 100% whether or not it receives any value from the Subscription Services post-termination. On an annual-advance invoicing model with auto-renewal, this creates multi-year effective lock-in. A 50% liquidated-damages structure compensates Lattice for lost contribution margin while preserving a meaningful exit right.
accepted_findings.position: 50% of remaining Fees as liquidated damages; structured as reasonable estimate of lost profit, not penalty.
accepted_findings.fallback: Two-thirds (66.67%) of remaining Fees as liquidated damages, or a sliding scale (75% if terminated in first six months of term, 50% thereafter).
accepted_findings.walkaway: 100% payment obligation with no substantive distinction from a non-cancelable contract.
accepted_findings.jurisdiction_assumed: Delaware; liquidated-damages provisions are enforceable if they bear a reasonable relationship to anticipated harm and are not punitive; Delaware courts apply a reasonableness standard at formation
accepted_findings.suggested_text: if Customer terminates this Agreement other than for Lattice's uncured material breach pursuant to Section 4.3, Customer shall pay, as liquidated damages and not as a penalty, fifty percent (50%) of the Fees that would have been payable for the remainder of the then-current Subscription Term
accepted_findings._rationale_explanation: Engages with the specific contract structure (annual-advance invoicing, auto-renewal) and articulates how the termination fee interacts with those terms to create multi-year lock-in, while proposing a calibrated remedy tied to lost margin.

accepted_findings.source_text: EXCEPT FOR (A) AMOUNTS OWED UNDER ANY ORDER FORM, (B) A PARTY'S BREACH OF SECTION 7 (CONFIDENTIALITY), AND (C) LATTICE'S OBLIGATIONS UNDER SECTION 8.3 (LATTICE IP INDEMNITY), IN NO EVENT WILL EITHER PARTY'S TOTAL CUMULATIVE LIABILITY ARISING OUT OF OR RELATED TO THIS AGREEMENT EXCEED THE FEES PAID OR PAYABLE BY CUSTOMER TO LATTICE UNDER THE APPLICABLE ORDER FORM IN THE TWELVE (12) MONTHS IMMEDIATELY PRECEDING THE EVENT GIVING RISE TO THE LIABILITY.
accepted_findings.proposed_text: EXCEPT FOR (A) AMOUNTS OWED UNDER ANY ORDER FORM, (B) A PARTY'S BREACH OF SECTION 7 (CONFIDENTIALITY), (C) LATTICE'S OBLIGATIONS UNDER SECTION 8.3 (LATTICE IP INDEMNITY), (D) EITHER PARTY'S GROSS NEGLIGENCE OR WILLFUL MISCONDUCT, AND (E) SECURITY INCIDENTS INVOLVING PERSONAL DATA (WHICH SHALL BE SUBJECT TO A SUPER-CAP OF THREE TIMES (3X) THE FEES PAID OR PAYABLE BY CUSTOMER TO LATTICE UNDER THE APPLICABLE ORDER FORM IN THE TWELVE (12) MONTHS IMMEDIATELY PRECEDING THE EVENT GIVING RISE TO THE LIABILITY, OR LATTICE'S AVAILABLE CYBER LIABILITY INSURANCE LIMITS, WHICHEVER IS GREATER), IN NO EVENT WILL EITHER PARTY'S TOTAL CUMULATIVE LIABILITY ARISING OUT OF OR RELATED TO THIS AGREEMENT EXCEED THE FEES PAID OR PAYABLE BY CUSTOMER TO LATTICE UNDER THE APPLICABLE ORDER FORM IN THE TWELVE (12) MONTHS IMMEDIATELY PRECEDING THE EVENT GIVING RISE TO THE LIABILITY.
accepted_findings.external_comment: The liability cap should exclude gross negligence and willful misconduct from its coverage, and should include a super-cap for Security Incidents involving personal data. This protects Customer's remedies for intentional or reckless conduct and ensures adequate recovery for data breach losses that could substantially exceed subscription fees.
accepted_findings.materiality_rationale: Without these carve-outs, Customer's remedies for vendor gross negligence or willful misconduct (including intentional data exfiltration, reckless security practices, or deliberate breach) are capped at 1x fees. A data breach involving personal data could trigger regulatory fines, forensic costs, notification obligations, and third-party claims totaling millions; capping Customer's recovery at 1x annual fees ($200K–$500K in a typical mid-market SaaS deal) leaves Customer catastrophically undercompensated.
accepted_findings.position: Add carve-outs: (D) either Party's gross negligence or willful misconduct (uncapped), and (E) Security Incidents involving personal data (super-cap at 3x fees or available cyber insurance limits, whichever is greater).
accepted_findings.fallback: Super-cap for data breaches at 2x fees or cyber insurance limits; accept cap on gross negligence if limited to non-intentional conduct and Customer retains uncapped remedy for willful misconduct and fraud.
accepted_findings.walkaway: Cap applies to data breaches involving personal data with no super-cap or insurance minimum, AND cap applies to willful misconduct or fraud.
accepted_findings.jurisdiction_assumed: Delaware (assumed based on Lattice incorporation state and absence of express governing law for liability cap enforceability). Gross negligence and willful misconduct carve-outs are enforceable in Delaware and most U.S. jurisdictions.
accepted_findings.suggested_text: EXCEPT FOR (A) AMOUNTS OWED UNDER ANY ORDER FORM, (B) A PARTY'S BREACH OF SECTION 7 (CONFIDENTIALITY), (C) LATTICE'S OBLIGATIONS UNDER SECTION 8.3 (LATTICE IP INDEMNITY), (D) EITHER PARTY'S GROSS NEGLIGENCE OR WILLFUL MISCONDUCT, AND (E) SECURITY INCIDENTS INVOLVING PERSONAL DATA (WHICH SHALL BE SUBJECT TO A SUPER-CAP OF THREE TIMES (3X) THE FEES PAID OR PAYABLE BY CUSTOMER TO LATTICE UNDER THE APPLICABLE ORDER FORM IN THE TWELVE (12) MONTHS IMMEDIATELY PRECEDING THE EVENT GIVING RISE TO THE LIABILITY, OR LATTICE'S AVAILABLE CYBER LIABILITY INSURANCE LIMITS, WHICHEVER IS GREATER), IN NO EVENT WILL EITHER PARTY'S TOTAL CUMULATIVE LIABILITY ARISING OUT OF OR RELATED TO THIS AGREEMENT EXCEED THE FEES PAID OR PAYABLE BY CUSTOMER TO LATTICE UNDER THE APPLICABLE ORDER FORM IN THE TWELVE (12) MONTHS IMMEDIATELY PRECEDING THE EVENT GIVING RISE TO THE LIABILITY.
accepted_findings._rationale_explanation: References general data breach consequences and typical mid-market deal values, but doesn't engage with this specific contract's structure, the $200K-$500K figure appears to be a general assumption rather than drawn from this deal, and doesn't analyze leverage or trade-offs given the 'their_paper_high_leverage' posture.

accepted_findings.proposed_text: 8.8 Customer IP Indemnity. Customer will defend Lattice against any claim, suit, or proceeding brought by a third party alleging that Customer Data, or any content, materials, or instructions provided by Customer to Lattice, directly infringes such third party's intellectual property rights (a "Customer IP Claim"), and will indemnify Lattice for any damages finally awarded against Lattice (or amounts paid in settlement approved by Customer) in connection with any Customer IP Claim. Customer's obligations under this Section 8.8 do not apply to any Customer IP Claim arising from: (a) Lattice's modification of Customer Data or customer-provided content without Customer's authorization; (b) Lattice's combination of Customer Data with any product, service, or data not provided or authorized by Customer; or (c) Lattice's use of Customer Data other than in accordance with this Agreement and Customer's instructions. The indemnification procedure set forth in Section 8.5 shall apply to Customer's obligations under this Section 8.8.
accepted_findings.external_comment: Customer should provide a reciprocal IP indemnity for third-party claims arising from Customer Data and customer-provided content. This establishes symmetry: Lattice indemnifies for the platform, Customer indemnifies for the data and materials Customer contributes.
accepted_findings.materiality_rationale: Lattice faces third-party IP risk from Customer Data (e.g., Customer uploads copyrighted works without authorization, Customer provides trade-secret-protected information from a competitor, Customer inputs patented algorithms). Without reciprocal indemnity, Lattice bears unilateral IP risk while Customer's IP risk is fully indemnified. Profile requires mutual IP indemnity; asymmetric structure is inconsistent with that position and leaves Customer exposed to leverage in negotiation.
accepted_findings.position: Add reciprocal Customer IP indemnity for third-party claims arising from Customer Data and customer-provided content, with standard carve-outs for unauthorized use or modification by Lattice.
accepted_findings.fallback: Customer IP indemnity limited to Customer's breach of its representation in Section 8.4 (that Customer has all necessary rights and consents for Customer Data), rather than direct infringement standard. Accept cap at 1x fees for Customer IP indemnity if Lattice IP indemnity is similarly capped (it is not — it is uncapped under Section 8.6).
accepted_findings.jurisdiction_assumed: Delaware. Mutual IP indemnities are market-standard in SaaS agreements and enforceable across U.S. jurisdictions.
accepted_findings.suggested_text: 8.8 Customer IP Indemnity. Customer will defend Lattice against any claim, suit, or proceeding brought by a third party alleging that Customer Data, or any content, materials, or instructions provided by Customer to Lattice, directly infringes such third party's intellectual property rights (a "Customer IP Claim"), and will indemnify Lattice for any damages finally awarded against Lattice (or amounts paid in settlement approved by Customer) in connection with any Customer IP Claim. Customer's obligations under this Section 8.8 do not apply to any Customer IP Claim arising from: (a) Lattice's modification of Customer Data or customer-provided content without Customer's authorization; (b) Lattice's combination of Customer Data with any product, service, or data not provided or authorized by Customer; or (c) Lattice's use of Customer Data other than in accordance with this Agreement and Customer's instructions. The indemnification procedure set forth in Section 8.5 shall apply to Customer's obligations under this Section 8.8.
accepted_findings._rationale_explanation: The rationale mechanically recites that the profile requires mutual IP indemnity and notes asymmetry exists, but provides no engagement with deal context, leverage position, contract structure, or deal-specific reasoning about whether this gap matters given the parties' actual roles and relationship.

accepted_findings.proposed_text: 8.9 Mutual Confidentiality Indemnity. Each Party (as indemnifying party) will defend the other Party (as indemnified party) against any third-party claim, suit, or proceeding alleging that the indemnifying Party's breach of Section 7 (Confidentiality) caused harm to such third party, and will indemnify the indemnified Party for any damages finally awarded against the indemnified Party (or amounts paid in settlement approved by the indemnifying Party) in connection with any such claim. The indemnification procedure set forth in Section 8.5 shall apply to each Party's obligations under this Section 8.9.
accepted_findings.external_comment: The Agreement should include mutual indemnification for third-party claims arising from confidentiality breaches, consistent with the mutual confidentiality obligations and the uncapped liability for confidentiality breach under Section 8.6.
accepted_findings.materiality_rationale: Section 8.6 uncaps liability for confidentiality breach, signaling that both Parties view such breaches as high-severity. Third-party claims (e.g., Customer's confidential information disclosed to a competitor; Lattice's confidential technical information disclosed to Customer's competitor) are a foreseeable consequence of confidentiality breach. Without indemnification, the breached party must pay its own defense costs and may face difficulties recovering if the breach harms a third party who sues the breached party rather than the breaching party. Profile requires mutual confidentiality indemnity.
accepted_findings.position: Add mutual indemnification for third-party claims arising from breach of confidentiality obligations.
accepted_findings.fallback: Accept language limiting indemnity to 'material' breaches of confidentiality (defined as breaches that result in disclosure to competitors or public disclosure).
accepted_findings.jurisdiction_assumed: Delaware. Confidentiality indemnities are standard and enforceable.
accepted_findings.suggested_text: 8.9 Mutual Confidentiality Indemnity. Each Party (as indemnifying party) will defend the other Party (as indemnified party) against any third-party claim, suit, or proceeding alleging that the indemnifying Party's breach of Section 7 (Confidentiality) caused harm to such third party, and will indemnify the indemnified Party for any damages finally awarded against the indemnified Party (or amounts paid in settlement approved by the indemnifying Party) in connection with any such claim. The indemnification procedure set forth in Section 8.5 shall apply to each Party's obligations under this Section 8.9.
accepted_findings._rationale_explanation: The rationale explicitly references Section 8.6's uncapped liability provision, connects it to the severity signal both parties embedded in this specific contract, and explains the structural gap where third-party claims fall outside the existing indemnification framework.

accepted_findings.source_text: Section 8.6 (liability cap); Section 9.2(b) (E&O/cyber insurance at $5M limits)
accepted_findings.external_comment: We note that Lattice's E&O and cyber insurance limits ($5M) substantially exceed the liability cap (1x fees, likely $200K–$500K annually for mid-market Customer). In the event of a claim exceeding the cap, Customer's recovery may be limited to the cap even where insurance coverage is available. Customer requests that Section 8.6 be amended to provide that the liability cap steps UP to available insurance limits for claims covered by Lattice's E&O or cyber policies, ensuring Customer can recover the benefit of insurance Lattice is required to maintain.
accepted_findings.materiality_rationale: Lattice is required to maintain $5M in cyber and E&O coverage, presumably to fund claims of that magnitude. The liability cap at 1x fees (likely $200K–$500K) means Customer cannot access 90%+ of that insurance even for covered claims. This misalignment defeats the purpose of the insurance requirement and leaves Customer undercompensated for losses Lattice is insured to pay. While not a Profile requirement, this cross-section issue represents a material gap in Customer's risk allocation.
accepted_findings.position: Amend Section 8.6 to provide that the liability cap shall be the greater of (i) 1x fees paid in the trailing 12 months, or (ii) the limits of Lattice's available E&O and cyber liability insurance for claims covered by such policies.
accepted_findings.fallback: Accept current cap structure if Lattice agrees to name Customer as an additional insured or loss payee on the E&O and cyber policies, granting Customer direct claim rights against the insurer for covered losses.
accepted_findings.jurisdiction_assumed: Delaware. Step-up provisions tying caps to insurance limits are enforceable and increasingly common in SaaS agreements.
accepted_findings._rationale_explanation: Engages with specific contract provisions (cross-references two sections), performs quantitative analysis of the insurance-to-cap gap ($5M vs. $200K-$500K), and explains how this contract's structure creates an internal inconsistency that undermines Customer's recovery rights.

accepted_findings.source_text: Any amounts not paid when due will accrue interest at the rate of one and one-half percent (1.5%) per month, or the maximum rate permitted by applicable law, whichever is lower, calculated from the date such payment was due until the date paid.
accepted_findings.proposed_text: Any amounts not paid when due will accrue interest at the rate of one percent (1.0%) per month, or the maximum rate permitted by applicable law, whichever is lower, calculated from the date such payment was due until the date paid.
accepted_findings.external_comment: A late payment interest rate of 1.5% per month (18% annually) is above market for customer-side payment terms in SaaS agreements. Customer proposes reducing the rate to 1.0% per month (12% annually), which is more consistent with commercial norms and Customer's cost of capital.
accepted_findings.materiality_rationale: 1.5% per month annualizes to 18%, which is high for B2B SaaS. Customer's Profile ceiling is 1% per month. On a $500K annual subscription paid net-60, a 30-day delay costs Customer $7,500 at 1.5% vs. $5,000 at 1.0%. The differential is modest but the Profile sets 1% as the standard.
accepted_findings.position: Reduce late payment interest to 1.0% per month.
accepted_findings.fallback: Accept 1.25% per month if Lattice offers a prompt-payment discount (e.g., 2% discount for payment within 10 days of invoice).
accepted_findings.jurisdiction_assumed: Delaware. Late payment interest rates are enforceable up to statutory usury limits (Delaware has no general usury statute for business loans; rates are generally enforceable as agreed). 1.0%–1.5% monthly rates are within enforceable range.
accepted_findings.suggested_text: Any amounts not paid when due will accrue interest at the rate of one percent (1.0%) per month, or the maximum rate permitted by applicable law, whichever is lower, calculated from the date such payment was due until the date paid.
accepted_findings._rationale_explanation: Applies the specific contract's payment terms (net-60, $500K annual value) to calculate concrete cost differentials under alternative rates, demonstrating how the Profile standard applies to this deal's financial structure.

accepted_findings.proposed_text: Customer shall be named as an additional insured on Lattice's errors and omissions / professional liability policy with respect to claims arising out of Lattice's performance of the Subscription Services and Professional Services under this Agreement.
accepted_findings.external_comment: To align insurance coverage with the indemnity obligations in Section 8.3, Customer should be named as an additional insured on the E&O policy. This ensures that Customer has direct recourse to Lattice's carrier in the event of an infringement or professional-services claim.
accepted_findings.materiality_rationale: Without additional-insured status on the E&O policy, Customer must pursue Lattice directly for indemnifiable claims under Section 8.3, creating collection risk if Lattice's assets are insufficient and forcing Customer to establish coverage entitlement through litigation rather than direct claim against the carrier.
accepted_findings.position: Customer named as additional insured on errors and omissions / professional liability policy for claims arising from Lattice's performance under this Agreement.
accepted_findings.fallback: Customer named as additional insured on E&O policy, but limited to claims where Lattice's indemnity obligation under Section 8.3 has been triggered.
accepted_findings.jurisdiction_assumed: Delaware (governing law per Section 10.1). Delaware law permits additional-insured endorsements on E&O policies and recognizes direct action rights where statutory or policy language grants them.
accepted_findings.suggested_text: Customer shall be named as an additional insured on Lattice's errors and omissions / professional liability policy with respect to claims arising out of Lattice's performance of the Subscription Services and Professional Services under this Agreement.
accepted_findings._rationale_explanation: The rationale engages with this specific contract by referencing Section 8.3's indemnity provisions and explains how the absence of additional-insured status creates collection risk tied to Lattice's asset sufficiency, showing reasoning grounded in the four corners of this agreement.

accepted_findings.proposed_text: Lattice shall provide, or cause its insurers to provide, Customer with at least thirty (30) days' prior written notice of any cancellation, non-renewal, or material reduction in the coverage limits or scope of the insurance required under Section 9.2.
accepted_findings.external_comment: Customer requires advance notice if the insurance coverage required under this Agreement is canceled, not renewed, or materially reduced. This allows Customer to assess whether to continue the engagement or require alternative risk mitigation.
accepted_findings.materiality_rationale: Without advance notice of policy cancellation or material change, Customer may continue performing under this Agreement believing that $5M E&O and $2M CGL coverage is in place when in fact coverage has lapsed, creating uninsured exposure to third-party claims Customer reasonably expected would be covered by Lattice's carrier.
accepted_findings.position: Thirty (30) days' prior written notice of cancellation, non-renewal, or material adverse change in required insurance.
accepted_findings.fallback: Ten (10) days' prior written notice for cancellation; thirty (30) days for non-renewal.
accepted_findings.jurisdiction_assumed: Delaware. Notice-of-cancellation provisions are standard and enforceable risk-allocation terms in commercial contracts governed by Delaware law.
accepted_findings.suggested_text: Lattice shall provide, or cause its insurers to provide, Customer with at least thirty (30) days' prior written notice of any cancellation, non-renewal, or material reduction in the coverage limits or scope of the insurance required under Section 9.2.
accepted_findings._rationale_explanation: Rationale engages with specific contract requirements ($5M E&O, $2M CGL) and explains concrete risk to Customer's reliance on those specific coverage amounts, though could strengthen by addressing leverage or next steps.

accepted_findings.source_text: During the Subscription Term, Lattice will maintain at its own expense, at minimum, the following insurance coverage with insurers authorized to do business in the United States:
accepted_findings.proposed_text: During the Subscription Term and for a period of three (3) years thereafter, Lattice will maintain at its own expense, at minimum, the following insurance coverage with insurers authorized to do business in the United States:
accepted_findings.external_comment: Insurance obligations should survive termination for a reasonable tail period. Claims arising from Lattice's performance during the Subscription Term may not manifest until after termination, particularly for E&O and cyber claims. A three-year post-termination tail is industry standard for SaaS engagements.
accepted_findings.materiality_rationale: Without a post-termination tail, Lattice may allow its E&O and CGL policies to lapse immediately upon termination, leaving Customer without recourse for claims arising from incidents that occurred during the Subscription Term but are discovered or asserted after termination (a common pattern for data-breach, infringement, and professional-error claims).
accepted_findings.position: Three (3) years post-termination survival of insurance obligations.
accepted_findings.fallback: Two (2) years post-termination, or Lattice may satisfy the requirement by purchasing extended reporting period (tail) coverage.
accepted_findings.jurisdiction_assumed: Delaware. Post-termination insurance survival provisions are standard risk-allocation terms enforceable under Delaware law.
accepted_findings.suggested_text: During the Subscription Term and for a period of three (3) years thereafter, Lattice will maintain at its own expense, at minimum, the following insurance coverage with insurers authorized to do business in the United States:
accepted_findings._rationale_explanation: References general principles about E&O/CGL tail coverage and common claim patterns, but doesn't engage with this specific contract's structure, the high-leverage deal posture, or whether other provisions (indemnification survival, limitation of liability) might mitigate the risk.

accepted_findings.proposed_text: To the extent permitted by its insurance policies without additional cost, Lattice waives, and will cause its insurers to waive, all rights of subrogation against Customer and its Affiliates with respect to any loss or damage covered by the insurance required under Section 9.2.
accepted_findings.external_comment: A mutual waiver of subrogation prevents insurers from pursuing the other party for losses that were covered by insurance. This avoids post-loss disputes in which Lattice's carrier sues Customer to recover amounts paid under Lattice's policy, or vice versa.
accepted_findings.materiality_rationale: Absent a waiver of subrogation, Lattice's CGL or E&O carrier may subrogate against Customer for losses Lattice's carrier paid on Lattice's behalf (for example, if Customer's act or omission contributed to the loss under comparative-fault principles). This creates surprise litigation exposure for Customer despite the existence of insurance intended to cover the claim.
accepted_findings.position: Mutual waiver of subrogation on all policies required under Section 9.2, to the extent permitted without additional premium.
accepted_findings.jurisdiction_assumed: Delaware. Waivers of subrogation are enforceable under Delaware law and do not violate public policy provided they do not impair the insurer's policy rights without consent.
accepted_findings.suggested_text: To the extent permitted by its insurance policies without additional cost, Lattice waives, and will cause its insurers to waive, all rights of subrogation against Customer and its Affiliates with respect to any loss or damage covered by the insurance required under Section 9.2.
accepted_findings._rationale_explanation: The rationale explains standard subrogation risk mechanics that apply to any commercial contract with insurance provisions, but does not engage with this specific contract's structure, the high-leverage deal posture, or Customer's specific risk profile under these terms.

accepted_findings.source_text: with insurers authorized to do business in the United States
accepted_findings.proposed_text: with insurers authorized to do business in the United States and rated A- or better by A.M. Best (or equivalent rating by a comparable rating agency)
accepted_findings.external_comment: Requiring carriers with a minimum financial-strength rating ensures that the required insurance is actually collectible in the event of a claim. An A- rating by A.M. Best is widely accepted as the baseline for financially sound commercial insurers.
accepted_findings.materiality_rationale: Without a financial-strength floor, Lattice could satisfy Section 9.2 by purchasing coverage from an undercapitalized or non-rated carrier. If a large claim arises and the carrier is insolvent or refuses to pay, the insurance requirement becomes illusory and Customer is forced to pursue Lattice directly, defeating the purpose of the insurance mandate.
accepted_findings.position: Insurers rated A- or better by A.M. Best or equivalent.
accepted_findings.jurisdiction_assumed: Delaware. Financial-rating requirements for insurers in commercial contracts are standard and enforceable under Delaware law.
accepted_findings.suggested_text: with insurers authorized to do business in the United States and rated A- or better by A.M. Best (or equivalent rating by a comparable rating agency)
accepted_findings._rationale_explanation: The rationale engages with this specific contract by naming the counterparty (Lattice), referencing the specific section number (9.2), analyzing how the current contract language creates a gap, and explaining the consequence to the client (Customer) under this deal structure.

accepted_findings.source_text: Upon Customer's written request, Lattice will provide a certificate of insurance evidencing such coverage.
accepted_findings.proposed_text: Lattice will provide a certificate of insurance evidencing such coverage upon execution of this Agreement and annually upon each policy renewal. Customer may request updated certificates at any time upon reasonable notice.
accepted_findings.external_comment: Automatic provision of certificates at policy inception and renewal eliminates the need for Customer to monitor policy expiration dates and request certificates reactively. This ensures continuous visibility into coverage status without administrative burden.
accepted_findings.materiality_rationale: Under the current 'upon request' standard, Customer must track Lattice's policy renewal dates and affirmatively request updated certificates, creating administrative overhead and the risk that a coverage lapse goes unnoticed. Automatic annual provision shifts the burden appropriately to the insured party and ensures Customer has timely notice of any coverage changes.
accepted_findings.position: Automatic provision of certificates at inception and annually upon renewal.
accepted_findings.jurisdiction_assumed: Delaware. Certificate-delivery mechanics are procedural contract terms enforceable under Delaware law.
accepted_findings.suggested_text: Lattice will provide a certificate of insurance evidencing such coverage upon execution of this Agreement and annually upon each policy renewal. Customer may request updated certificates at any time upon reasonable notice.
accepted_findings._rationale_explanation: The rationale engages with this specific contract's 'upon request' language and explains the operational burden it creates for Customer in this deal structure, contrasting it with the proposed automatic provision mechanism.

accepted_findings.source_text: Lattice's sole obligations and Customer's sole and exclusive remedies for failures to meet such service-level commitments are the service credits described in Exhibit A.
accepted_findings.proposed_text: Lattice's obligations for failures to meet the service-level commitments set forth in Exhibit A include the service credits described in Exhibit A. Such service credits do not limit Customer's other rights and remedies under this Agreement, including the right to terminate for material breach under Section 4.3 if Lattice repeatedly fails to meet the service-level commitments.
accepted_findings.external_comment: Framing SLA credits as the 'sole and exclusive remedy' prevents Customer from exercising standard termination rights in the event of persistent service failures. We propose making the credits available without limiting other contractual remedies.
accepted_findings.materiality_rationale: If Lattice experiences chronic availability failures that make the Subscription Services unusable for Customer's business, Customer would be limited to modest service credits and unable to terminate for breach even if the failures are material and repeated. This creates a locked-in exposure where Customer pays for a non-functioning service.
accepted_findings.position: Delete 'sole and exclusive' language; clarify that SLA credits do not limit termination rights for material and repeated SLA failures.
accepted_findings.fallback: Retain 'sole and exclusive' framing only for single isolated SLA failures; permit termination if SLA failures occur in three or more consecutive measurement periods.
accepted_findings.jurisdiction_assumed: Delaware (assumed from Section 10.1 governing law). Under Delaware law, contractual sole-remedy provisions are generally enforceable but may be subject to failure-of-essential-purpose analysis. Customer needs the ability to exit if the service becomes non-functional.
accepted_findings.suggested_text: Lattice's obligations for failures to meet the service-level commitments set forth in Exhibit A include the service credits described in Exhibit A. Such service credits do not limit Customer's other rights and remedies under this Agreement, including the right to terminate for material breach under Section 4.3 if Lattice repeatedly fails to meet the service-level commitments.
accepted_findings._rationale_explanation: The rationale engages with this specific contract by connecting the 'sole and exclusive' remedy language to Customer's position as a paying subscriber who could be locked into a non-functioning service, demonstrating understanding of how this provision operates within the SaaS subscription structure.

accepted_findings.source_text: Section 2.3 in combination with Section 8.6
accepted_findings.external_comment: Section 2.3 states that service credits are Customer's 'sole and exclusive remedy' for SLA failures. Section 8.6 caps liability at fees paid in the trailing 12 months. If Exhibit A contains uncapped service credits, these two provisions create an internal contradiction. We request clarification that service credits are subject to the liability cap stated in Section 8.6, or that Section 2.3 be revised to remove the sole-remedy language.
accepted_findings.materiality_rationale: If Exhibit A provides service credits that could theoretically exceed the 12-month fees-paid liability cap (e.g., 100% refund for extended outages), the contract contains contradictory remedy frameworks. This ambiguity will lead to disputes in the event of a major outage. Customer needs clarity on whether the liability cap governs all remedies or whether service credits are uncapped.
accepted_findings.position: Clarify that Section 8.6 liability cap governs all remedies including service credits, OR remove 'sole and exclusive' language from Section 2.3 to permit damages beyond credits in cases of gross negligence or material breach.
accepted_findings.fallback: Cap service credits at the Section 8.6 limit but exclude the cap for willful misconduct or gross negligence causing SLA failures.
accepted_findings.jurisdiction_assumed: Delaware (assumed from Section 10.1). Delaware courts enforce contractual remedy limitations but require internal consistency. The current drafting creates ambiguity as to the hierarchy of remedy provisions.
accepted_findings._rationale_explanation: Engages with the specific contract structure by identifying a concrete contradiction between two sections (2.3's 'sole and exclusive' language and 8.6's liability cap) and reasoning about how this ambiguity creates dispute risk in the outage scenario, though it stops short of weighing whether to push given the high leverage posture.

accepted_findings.source_text: Lattice may terminate this Agreement or any Order Form upon written notice to Customer if Customer materially breaches this Agreement and fails to cure such breach within thirty (30) days after receipt of written notice describing the breach in reasonable detail. Customer may terminate this Agreement or any Order Form upon written notice to Lattice if Lattice materially breaches this Agreement and fails to cure such breach within sixty (60) days after receipt of written notice describing the breach in reasonable detail.
accepted_findings.proposed_text: Either Party may terminate this Agreement or any Order Form upon written notice to the other Party if the other Party materially breaches this Agreement and fails to cure such breach within thirty (30) days after receipt of written notice describing the breach in reasonable detail.
accepted_findings.external_comment: The current cure periods are asymmetric: Customer has 30 days to cure breaches, while Lattice has 60 days. We request symmetric cure periods to ensure parity in remedies and obligations.
accepted_findings.materiality_rationale: The 2x disparity in cure periods gives Lattice twice as long to remedy its failures (e.g., persistent service outages, security incidents, data loss) while Customer faces accelerated termination risk for payment delays or other breaches. In a high-leverage deal where Customer needs the service, this asymmetry compounds Customer's bargaining disadvantage. A payment breach is typically cured in days, not 30 days, so the disparity is not justified by the nature of likely breaches.
accepted_findings.position: 30-day mutual cure period for all material breaches.
accepted_findings.fallback: 30 days for payment and confidentiality breaches (both sides); 45 days for other material breaches (both sides).
accepted_findings.jurisdiction_assumed: Delaware (assumed from Section 10.1). Symmetric cure periods are market-standard in negotiated SaaS agreements; asymmetry of this magnitude favors the vendor beyond what is typical.
accepted_findings.suggested_text: Either Party may terminate this Agreement or any Order Form upon written notice to the other Party if the other Party materially breaches this Agreement and fails to cure such breach within thirty (30) days after receipt of written notice describing the breach in reasonable detail.
accepted_findings._rationale_explanation: Engages with the specific deal posture (high-leverage, customer's disadvantage), analyzes the asymmetry's practical impact on different breach types (service outages vs. payment delays), and explains why the disparity isn't justified by the nature of likely breaches under this contract.

accepted_findings.source_text: LATTICE DOES NOT WARRANT THAT THE SUBSCRIPTION SERVICES WILL BE UNINTERRUPTED, ERROR-FREE, OR FREE OF HARMFUL COMPONENTS, OR THAT CUSTOMER DATA WILL BE SECURE OR NOT OTHERWISE LOST OR DAMAGED.
accepted_findings.proposed_text: LATTICE DOES NOT WARRANT THAT THE SUBSCRIPTION SERVICES WILL BE UNINTERRUPTED OR ERROR-FREE, EXCEPT AS EXPRESSLY PROVIDED IN THE SERVICE LEVEL SCHEDULE (EXHIBIT A).
accepted_findings.external_comment: Section 8.2 disclaims all warranties related to service interruption and data security, but Section 2.3 references service-level commitments in Exhibit A (which typically include uptime and availability warranties), and Section 5.2 warrants a SOC 2 Type II security program. The blanket disclaimer creates ambiguity as to whether the SLA commitments and security program representations are enforceable. We propose narrowing the disclaimer to preserve the SLA and security commitments.
accepted_findings.materiality_rationale: If Section 8.2's disclaimer is interpreted to override the SLA commitments in Exhibit A and the SOC 2 representation in Section 5.2, Customer has no enforceable performance standard for availability or security. This makes Sections 2.3 and 5.2 illusory. In the event of a major outage or security failure, Lattice could argue the disclaimer negates any obligation. Customer needs the SLA and security program to be binding.
accepted_findings.position: Delete 'uninterrupted, error-free' disclaimer language entirely; allow Section 2.3 SLA and Section 5.2 security program to govern without contradiction.
accepted_findings.fallback: Narrow disclaimer to: 'except as expressly provided in the Service Level Schedule (Exhibit A), Lattice does not warrant that the Subscription Services will be uninterrupted or error-free.'
accepted_findings.jurisdiction_assumed: Delaware (assumed from Section 10.1). Delaware courts apply the doctrine of reasonable expectations and may harmonize conflicting provisions by limiting broad disclaimers where specific affirmative warranties exist.
accepted_findings.suggested_text: LATTICE DOES NOT WARRANT THAT THE SUBSCRIPTION SERVICES WILL BE UNINTERRUPTED OR ERROR-FREE, EXCEPT AS EXPRESSLY PROVIDED IN THE SERVICE LEVEL SCHEDULE (EXHIBIT A).
accepted_findings._rationale_explanation: Engages with specific contract sections (8.2, 2.3, 5.2, Exhibit A) and articulates how the disclaimer could render other provisions illusory, grounding the risk analysis in this contract's four corners rather than generic warranty principles.

accepted_findings.proposed_text: 2.6 Customer Obligations; Delay Relief. Customer will provide Lattice with timely access to personnel, systems, information, and approvals reasonably necessary for Lattice to perform its obligations under this Agreement. If Customer's failure to provide such cooperation causes a delay in Lattice's performance, (a) such delay will not constitute a breach by Lattice, (b) any performance timelines and deadlines will be extended by the period of Customer-caused delay, and (c) Lattice will not be liable for any failure to meet service-level commitments to the extent caused by Customer's failure to cooperate.
accepted_findings.external_comment: The Agreement is silent on how Customer-caused delays (e.g., failure to provide required information, untimely approvals, system access delays) affect Lattice's performance obligations. We propose adding a provision that extends performance timelines and excuses SLA failures to the extent caused by Customer's failure to cooperate.
accepted_findings.materiality_rationale: In Professional Services engagements and complex SaaS implementations, Customer delays are common (approval cycles, resource availability, data provisioning). Without a delay-relief provision, Lattice remains liable for missing deadlines and SLA targets even when the delay is Customer-caused. If Customer withholds critical information for weeks and then claims an SLA breach, Lattice has no contractual defense. This creates asymmetric risk where Lattice is liable for factors outside its control.
accepted_findings.position: Add mutual dependency provision excusing both Parties' performance delays to the extent caused by the other Party's failure to fulfill its dependencies.
accepted_findings.fallback: Add unilateral Lattice relief for Customer-caused delays, but limit the scope to Professional Services timelines (not SLA uptime measurements).
accepted_findings.jurisdiction_assumed: Delaware (assumed from Section 10.1). Under common-law principles, a party's performance obligations may be excused when the other party's failure to cooperate prevents performance, but explicit contractual language is preferable.
accepted_findings.suggested_text: 2.6 Customer Obligations; Delay Relief. Customer will provide Lattice with timely access to personnel, systems, information, and approvals reasonably necessary for Lattice to perform its obligations under this Agreement. If Customer's failure to provide such cooperation causes a delay in Lattice's performance, (a) such delay will not constitute a breach by Lattice, (b) any performance timelines and deadlines will be extended by the period of Customer-caused delay, and (c) Lattice will not be liable for any failure to meet service-level commitments to the extent caused by Customer's failure to cooperate.
accepted_findings._rationale_explanation: References common industry scenarios (approval cycles, resource availability) and standard SLA risk, but doesn't engage with this specific contract's structure, the client's high-leverage buyer position, or whether Lattice's existing terms already address dependencies.

accepted_findings.source_text: Neither Party will be liable for any delay or failure to perform its obligations under this Agreement (other than payment obligations) to the extent such delay or failure is caused by an event beyond such Party's reasonable control, including without limitation acts of God, natural disasters, war, terrorism, civil unrest, pandemic, government action, labor disputes, or failures of third-party telecommunications, internet, or utility providers (a "Force Majeure Event").
accepted_findings.proposed_text: Neither Party will be liable for any delay or failure to perform its obligations under this Agreement (other than payment obligations) to the extent such delay or failure is caused by an event beyond such Party's reasonable control, including without limitation acts of God, natural disasters, war, terrorism, civil unrest, pandemic, government action, labor disputes, or failures of third-party telecommunications, internet, or utility providers (a "Force Majeure Event"). For the avoidance of doubt, Force Majeure Events will be excluded from the calculation of service-level compliance under the Service Level Schedule (Exhibit A), and no service credits will accrue during Force Majeure Events.
accepted_findings.external_comment: Section 9.3 excuses performance during Force Majeure Events but does not address whether such events are excluded from SLA calculations. If a pandemic or provider outage causes service unavailability, the interaction between Section 9.3 and Section 2.3 is ambiguous. We propose clarifying that Force Majeure Events do not count against SLA compliance and do not generate service credits.
accepted_findings.materiality_rationale: Without explicit clarification, Customer could argue that Lattice owes service credits for downtime during Force Majeure Events (e.g., a regional internet provider failure), even though Section 9.3 states Lattice is not 'liable' for the failure. The term 'liable' in Section 9.3 is ambiguous as to whether it excludes SLA credit obligations. If Customer demands credits for pandemic-related outages, Lattice faces a colorable dispute. Clarifying the interaction avoids litigation risk and aligns with market-standard SLA exclusions.
accepted_findings.position: Add explicit statement that Force Majeure Events are excluded from SLA calculations and do not trigger service credits.
accepted_findings.jurisdiction_assumed: Delaware (assumed from Section 10.1). Market-standard SLA schedules exclude Force Majeure Events from uptime calculations. The absence of explicit language creates ambiguity.
accepted_findings.suggested_text: Neither Party will be liable for any delay or failure to perform its obligations under this Agreement (other than payment obligations) to the extent such delay or failure is caused by an event beyond such Party's reasonable control, including without limitation acts of God, natural disasters, war, terrorism, civil unrest, pandemic, government action, labor disputes, or failures of third-party telecommunications, internet, or utility providers (a "Force Majeure Event"). For the avoidance of doubt, Force Majeure Events will be excluded from the calculation of service-level compliance under the Service Level Schedule (Exhibit A), and no service credits will accrue during Force Majeure Events.
accepted_findings._rationale_explanation: Engages with specific contract structure by identifying the ambiguity between Section 9.3's 'liable' language and SLA credit obligations, showing how this specific contract creates a gap that could lead to a concrete dispute scenario.

accepted_findings.proposed_text: If an SOW does not specify acceptance criteria for a deliverable, the deliverable will be deemed accepted unless Customer provides written notice of non-conformance with the SOW specifications within ten (10) business days after delivery, specifying in reasonable detail the nature of the non-conformance. Lattice will use commercially reasonable efforts to correct any material non-conformance and redeliver the corrected deliverable. If Lattice is unable to correct the non-conformance within thirty (30) days after receiving Customer's notice, Customer may, as its sole remedy, terminate the applicable SOW and receive a refund of fees paid for the non-conforming deliverable.
accepted_findings.external_comment: Section 2.5 delegates acceptance criteria to individual SOWs but does not provide a default acceptance process for SOWs that omit acceptance terms. We propose adding a deemed-acceptance provision to ensure clarity when SOWs are silent.
accepted_findings.materiality_rationale: If an SOW is signed without explicit acceptance criteria (common in time-and-materials engagements), the Agreement is silent on when Lattice's obligation is complete and when payment is due. Customer could withhold payment indefinitely claiming the deliverable does not meet unstated expectations. Lattice has no mechanism to force closure. A default deemed-acceptance provision prevents indefinite limbo and aligns with market-standard consulting terms.
accepted_findings.position: Add deemed-acceptance provision with 10-business-day review period and cure process.
accepted_findings.fallback: Add deemed-acceptance provision with 20-business-day review period.
accepted_findings.jurisdiction_assumed: Delaware (assumed from Section 10.1). Delaware law recognizes deemed-acceptance provisions as enforceable when parties fail to specify acceptance terms. This is a gap-filling provision.
accepted_findings.suggested_text: If an SOW does not specify acceptance criteria for a deliverable, the deliverable will be deemed accepted unless Customer provides written notice of non-conformance with the SOW specifications within ten (10) business days after delivery, specifying in reasonable detail the nature of the non-conformance. Lattice will use commercially reasonable efforts to correct any material non-conformance and redeliver the corrected deliverable. If Lattice is unable to correct the non-conformance within thirty (30) days after receiving Customer's notice, Customer may, as its sole remedy, terminate the applicable SOW and receive a refund of fees paid for the non-conforming deliverable.
accepted_findings._rationale_explanation: References a general consulting industry problem (indefinite payment withholding) and cites 'market-standard consulting terms' but does not engage with this specific contract's payment terms, cure provisions, dispute resolution mechanisms, or whether high leverage as a customer makes this a worthwhile fight on their paper.

accepted_findings.category: termination for breach - asymmetric cure periods
accepted_findings.source_text: Lattice may terminate this Agreement or any Order Form upon written notice to Customer if Customer materially breaches this Agreement and fails to cure such breach within thirty (30) days after receipt of written notice describing the breach in reasonable detail. Customer may terminate this Agreement or any Order Form upon written notice to Lattice if Lattice materially breaches this Agreement and fails to cure such breach within sixty (60) days after receipt of written notice describing the breach in reasonable detail.
accepted_findings.proposed_text: Either Party may terminate this Agreement or any Order Form upon written notice to the other Party if the other Party materially breaches this Agreement and fails to cure such breach within thirty (30) days after receipt of written notice describing the breach in reasonable detail.
accepted_findings.external_comment: The current cure period structure gives Lattice twice the cure time that Customer receives (60 days vs 30 days). Symmetric cure periods are standard in commercial SaaS agreements and ensure parity in the parties' ability to remedy breaches before termination.
accepted_findings.materiality_rationale: Asymmetric cure periods mean Customer can be terminated for breach in half the time Lattice can, creating operational risk where Customer has minimal time to address alleged breaches (e.g., payment disputes, access-control issues) while Lattice enjoys double the remediation window for service failures, security incidents, or data-handling violations.
accepted_findings.position: Symmetric 30-day cure periods for both parties.
accepted_findings.fallback: Symmetric 60-day cure periods for both parties.
accepted_findings.jurisdiction_assumed: Delaware (governing law per Section 10.1)
accepted_findings.suggested_text: Either Party may terminate this Agreement or any Order Form upon written notice to the other Party if the other Party materially breaches this Agreement and fails to cure such breach within thirty (30) days after receipt of written notice describing the breach in reasonable detail.
accepted_findings._rationale_explanation: References general operational risks and example breach types, but doesn't engage with this specific contract's structure, the high-leverage deal posture, or whether the stated 30-day symmetric cure period contradicts the finding's category label.

accepted_findings.source_text: Unless otherwise specified in an Order Form, upon expiration of the Initial Term, the Subscription Services will automatically renew for successive one-year periods (each, a "Renewal Term") unless either Party provides written notice of non-renewal to the other Party at least sixty (60) days prior to the end of the then-current Subscription Term.
accepted_findings.proposed_text: Unless otherwise specified in an Order Form, upon expiration of the Initial Term, the Subscription Services will automatically renew for successive one-year periods (each, a "Renewal Term") unless either Party provides written notice of non-renewal to the other Party at least thirty (30) days prior to the end of the then-current Subscription Term.
accepted_findings.external_comment: A 60-day non-renewal notice window creates calendar risk for both parties. Thirty days aligns with standard procurement and budget-approval cycles and is market-standard for SaaS subscriptions of this size.
accepted_findings.materiality_rationale: Customer's procurement cycle typically operates on 30-day notice windows; missing the 60-day cutoff by even a few days locks Customer into an additional one-year term and fees that may no longer align with business needs, technology strategy, or budget availability.
accepted_findings.position: 30-day non-renewal notice.
accepted_findings.fallback: 45-day non-renewal notice.
accepted_findings.jurisdiction_assumed: Delaware (governing law per Section 10.1)
accepted_findings.suggested_text: Unless otherwise specified in an Order Form, upon expiration of the Initial Term, the Subscription Services will automatically renew for successive one-year periods (each, a "Renewal Term") unless either Party provides written notice of non-renewal to the other Party at least thirty (30) days prior to the end of the then-current Subscription Term.
accepted_findings._rationale_explanation: References general procurement timing concerns that could apply to any customer with standard 30-day cycles, but doesn't engage with this specific contract's structure, the deal posture (high leverage on their paper), or weigh whether the 60-day term is visible in the source text.

accepted_findings.source_text: The arbitration will be conducted in San Francisco, California by a single arbitrator selected in accordance with such rules.
accepted_findings.proposed_text: The arbitration will be conducted in the state where Customer's principal place of business is located, or in a location mutually agreed by the parties, by a single arbitrator selected in accordance with such rules.
accepted_findings.external_comment: Exclusive arbitration in San Francisco imposes travel and counsel costs on Customer for every dispute, regardless of size or subject matter. Customer-state or mutually agreed neutral location is market-standard for agreements involving parties headquartered in different regions.
accepted_findings.materiality_rationale: For disputes under $100K, the cost of retaining California-based arbitration counsel and traveling for hearings may exceed the amount in controversy, effectively denying Customer a practical remedy for smaller disputes and creating structural advantage for Lattice in settlement negotiations.
accepted_findings.position: Arbitration in Customer's home state.
accepted_findings.fallback: Arbitration in Delaware (the governing-law jurisdiction) as a neutral location.
accepted_findings.jurisdiction_assumed: Delaware (governing law per Section 10.1)
accepted_findings.suggested_text: The arbitration will be conducted in the state where Customer's principal place of business is located, or in a location mutually agreed by the parties, by a single arbitrator selected in accordance with such rules.
accepted_findings._rationale_explanation: Engages with the specific contract's arbitration structure by analyzing the practical economic consequence of the California venue requirement against a specific dollar threshold ($100K), showing how the forum selection creates asymmetric leverage in this particular dispute-resolution framework.

accepted_findings.source_text: if Customer terminates this Agreement other than for Lattice's uncured material breach pursuant to Section 4.3, Customer shall pay all Fees that would have been payable for the remainder of the then-current Subscription Term
accepted_findings.proposed_text: if Customer terminates this Agreement other than for Lattice's uncured material breach pursuant to Section 4.3, Customer shall pay Fees accrued through the effective date of termination
accepted_findings.external_comment: Requiring payment of all future fees for the remainder of the term, even after termination, effectively eliminates Customer's ability to exit the Agreement for non-breach reasons. This is inconsistent with the treatment of termination rights and creates a financial lock-in that is not standard for subscription agreements.
accepted_findings.materiality_rationale: If Customer needs to terminate mid-term for business reasons (e.g., acquisition, change in technology strategy, budget reduction), Customer remains liable for the full multi-year fee commitment with no corresponding service delivery. In a high-leverage deal this may be non-negotiable, but the language should be explicit and Customer should understand the exposure.
accepted_findings.position: Customer pays only fees accrued through termination date.
accepted_findings.fallback: Customer pays a termination fee equal to 50% of remaining fees, or three months' fees, whichever is less.
accepted_findings.jurisdiction_assumed: Delaware (governing law per Section 10.1)
accepted_findings.suggested_text: if Customer terminates this Agreement other than for Lattice's uncured material breach pursuant to Section 4.3, Customer shall pay Fees accrued through the effective date of termination
accepted_findings._rationale_explanation: Engages with specific deal posture (high-leverage acknowledgment), articulates concrete business scenarios triggering the risk, and explicitly weighs the exposure against negotiability given Customer's leverage position.

accepted_findings.source_text: for thirty (30) days following termination, Lattice will, upon Customer's written request and at Customer's expense, make Customer Data available for download in a commercially reasonable format.
accepted_findings.proposed_text: for sixty (60) days following termination, Lattice will, upon Customer's written request and at no additional charge to Customer, make Customer Data available for download in a commercially reasonable format (including CSV, JSON, or other machine-readable format).
accepted_findings.external_comment: A 30-day retrieval window may be insufficient for Customer to stand up an alternative system, complete data migration, and validate data integrity, particularly for large datasets. Sixty days is more aligned with typical enterprise migration timelines, and Customer should not bear additional cost to retrieve its own data.
accepted_findings.materiality_rationale: If Customer's migration project encounters delays (vendor onboarding, technical integration issues, internal resource constraints), a 30-day window creates risk of permanent data loss. Charging Customer for retrieval of its own data post-termination adds cost to an already disruptive exit process.
accepted_findings.position: 60-day retrieval window at no additional charge; machine-readable format specified.
accepted_findings.fallback: 45-day retrieval window at no additional charge.
accepted_findings.jurisdiction_assumed: Delaware (governing law per Section 10.1)
accepted_findings.suggested_text: for sixty (60) days following termination, Lattice will, upon Customer's written request and at no additional charge to Customer, make Customer Data available for download in a commercially reasonable format (including CSV, JSON, or other machine-readable format).
accepted_findings._rationale_explanation: Engages with specific contract terms (30-day window, at Customer's expense) and articulates concrete operational risks tied to this deal structure, though stops short of weighing whether to push given the high leverage posture.

accepted_findings.proposed_text: Actions seeking equitable relief may be brought in any court of competent jurisdiction in the state where the defendant is headquartered.
accepted_findings.external_comment: The arbitration clause carves out actions seeking equitable relief but does not specify venue for such actions, creating ambiguity about where a party may seek injunctive relief for breach of confidentiality, IP misuse, or data security incidents. Clarifying venue avoids jurisdictional disputes in time-sensitive matters.
accepted_findings.materiality_rationale: If Customer needs to seek emergency injunctive relief (e.g., to stop Lattice from disclosing Customer Data in violation of confidentiality obligations), uncertainty about proper venue can delay relief and increase legal costs while parties litigate threshold jurisdictional questions.
accepted_findings.position: Equitable relief venue in the jurisdiction of the defendant.
accepted_findings.fallback: Equitable relief venue in Delaware (governing-law jurisdiction).
accepted_findings.jurisdiction_assumed: Delaware (governing law per Section 10.1)
accepted_findings.suggested_text: Actions seeking equitable relief may be brought in any court of competent jurisdiction in the state where the defendant is headquartered.
accepted_findings._rationale_explanation: Describes general risks of venue uncertainty for injunctive relief but doesn't engage with this specific contract's structure, the high leverage position, or whether this issue is worth negotiating given the deal posture.

accepted_findings.source_text: The obligations of confidentiality set forth in this Section 7 will continue during the Subscription Term and for a period of three (3) years thereafter, except that with respect to trade secrets such obligations will continue for as long as the information qualifies as a trade secret under applicable law.
accepted_findings.proposed_text: The obligations of confidentiality set forth in this Section 7 will continue during the Subscription Term and for a period of five (5) years thereafter, except that with respect to trade secrets such obligations will continue for as long as the information qualifies as a trade secret under applicable law.
accepted_findings.external_comment: A three-year post-termination confidentiality tail is shorter than market for commercial information in the SaaS industry. We propose extending the tail to five years to reflect the typical lifecycle of competitive and commercial information.
accepted_findings.materiality_rationale: Customer may disclose sensitive business strategies, integration plans, and commercial terms that retain competitive value beyond three years. A three-year tail creates exposure if Lattice were acquired by a competitor or if the relationship ends contentiously and Lattice personnel retain access to Customer's confidential information during a period when it remains commercially sensitive.
accepted_findings.position: Five-year post-termination confidentiality tail for non-trade-secret confidential information.
accepted_findings.fallback: Four-year tail with enhanced carve-out ensuring trade secrets remain protected indefinitely.
accepted_findings.jurisdiction_assumed: Delaware (stated in 10.1). Delaware does not prescribe confidentiality duration; five years is standard commercial practice.
accepted_findings.suggested_text: The obligations of confidentiality set forth in this Section 7 will continue during the Subscription Term and for a period of five (5) years thereafter, except that with respect to trade secrets such obligations will continue for as long as the information qualifies as a trade secret under applicable law.
accepted_findings._rationale_explanation: Engages with the specific client role (customer disclosing sensitive information to vendor), discusses concrete scenarios specific to this relationship (Lattice acquisition by competitor, contentious termination), and explains why the standard three-year tail is insufficient given what the customer will be disclosing.

accepted_findings.proposed_text: 6.4 Customer Outputs. Customer shall own all right, title, and interest in and to any reports, dashboards, analyses, visualizations, and other work product or deliverables specifically created for Customer under this Agreement or any Order Form or SOW (collectively, 'Customer Outputs'). Lattice hereby assigns to Customer all such right, title, and interest. Lattice retains ownership of the underlying Subscription Services, methodologies, tools, and any pre-existing or independently developed materials used to create Customer Outputs, and Customer receives a perpetual, irrevocable license to use any such underlying materials solely as embodied in the Customer Outputs.
accepted_findings.external_comment: The Agreement does not address ownership of custom reports, dashboards, or analytics outputs created specifically for Customer. Industry practice is that customer-specific deliverables, as distinct from the platform itself, belong to the customer commissioning them. We propose adding Section 6.4 to clarify that Customer owns work product created for its specific use, while Lattice retains the underlying platform and tools.
accepted_findings.materiality_rationale: Without clarity on ownership of customer-specific outputs (reports, custom dashboards, analytics), Customer risks losing access to business-critical deliverables upon termination or dispute. If Lattice treats these outputs as its IP, Customer may be unable to migrate insights, demonstrate compliance to auditors, or use the outputs with a replacement vendor. This is particularly material if Customer commissions Professional Services to build custom analytics.
accepted_findings.position: Customer owns all custom reports, dashboards, and analytics outputs specifically created for Customer. Lattice retains platform IP and general methodologies.
accepted_findings.fallback: Joint ownership of customer-specific outputs, with each party having the right to use, modify, and commercialize. Customer receives a perpetual, irrevocable, royalty-free license.
accepted_findings.jurisdiction_assumed: Delaware (stated in 10.1). Under Delaware common law, absent express assignment, the creator (Lattice) would own deliverables, which is contrary to Customer's interests.
accepted_findings.suggested_text: 6.4 Customer Outputs. Customer shall own all right, title, and interest in and to any reports, dashboards, analyses, visualizations, and other work product or deliverables specifically created for Customer under this Agreement or any Order Form or SOW (collectively, 'Customer Outputs'). Lattice hereby assigns to Customer all such right, title, and interest. Lattice retains ownership of the underlying Subscription Services, methodologies, tools, and any pre-existing or independently developed materials used to create Customer Outputs, and Customer receives a perpetual, irrevocable license to use any such underlying materials solely as embodied in the Customer Outputs.
accepted_findings._rationale_explanation: Articulates standard risks of IP ambiguity in SaaS contracts but doesn't engage with the actual contract text showing Customer already owns custom outputs, nor with the high-leverage posture that could push for broader rights.

accepted_findings.source_text: Lattice retains all right, title, and interest in any methodology, know-how, tools, and pre-existing materials used in connection with Professional Services.
accepted_findings.proposed_text: Lattice retains all right, title, and interest in any methodology, know-how, tools, and pre-existing materials used in connection with Professional Services. Custom deliverables created specifically for Customer under a Statement of Work (including configurations, integrations, custom reports, training materials, and documentation) shall be owned by Customer, subject to Lattice's retention of ownership in the underlying Subscription Services and any general-purpose tools or methodologies. Lattice hereby assigns to Customer all right, title, and interest in such custom deliverables upon full payment of the applicable Fees.
accepted_findings.external_comment: Section 2.5 addresses Lattice's retention of general methodologies but does not clarify ownership of custom deliverables created specifically for Customer under an SOW. Standard practice is that bespoke work product commissioned by the customer belongs to the customer, while the service provider retains its general tools and know-how. We propose clarifying this allocation.
accepted_findings.materiality_rationale: If Lattice owns all Professional Services deliverables, Customer cannot independently use or modify custom integrations, configurations, or training materials after the relationship ends. This creates vendor lock-in and forces Customer to repurchase work it has already paid for if it transitions to a different platform. The absence of clarity here materially undermines the value of the Professional Services spend.
accepted_findings.position: Customer owns all custom deliverables created specifically for Customer under an SOW. Lattice retains general methodologies and platform IP.
accepted_findings.fallback: Lattice grants Customer a perpetual, irrevocable, royalty-free license to use all Professional Services deliverables, with the right to modify and create derivative works.
