accepted_findings.jurisdiction_assumed: Delaware (stated in 10.1). Under Delaware law, absent express assignment, the service provider owns deliverables it creates, which is disadvantageous to Customer.
accepted_findings.suggested_text: Lattice retains all right, title, and interest in any methodology, know-how, tools, and pre-existing materials used in connection with Professional Services. Custom deliverables created specifically for Customer under a Statement of Work (including configurations, integrations, custom reports, training materials, and documentation) shall be owned by Customer, subject to Lattice's retention of ownership in the underlying Subscription Services and any general-purpose tools or methodologies. Lattice hereby assigns to Customer all right, title, and interest in such custom deliverables upon full payment of the applicable Fees.
accepted_findings._rationale_explanation: The rationale articulates standard vendor lock-in concerns that would apply to any professional services arrangement, but doesn't engage with this contract's actual structure—which already gives Customer ownership of custom deliverables per the 'position' field, making the concern potentially moot.

accepted_findings.source_text: If Customer or its Authorized Users provide Lattice with any suggestions, recommendations, ideas, or feedback regarding the Subscription Services or the business of Lattice ('Feedback'), Customer hereby grants Lattice a non-exclusive, perpetual, irrevocable, worldwide, royalty-free, transferable, sublicensable right and license to use, copy, modify, distribute, and otherwise exploit such Feedback for any purpose, without any obligation or compensation to Customer.
accepted_findings.proposed_text: If Customer or its Authorized Users provide Lattice with any suggestions, recommendations, ideas, or feedback regarding the Subscription Services ('Feedback'), Customer hereby grants Lattice a non-exclusive, perpetual, irrevocable, worldwide, royalty-free right and license to use, copy, modify, and incorporate such Feedback into the Subscription Services and related products, without any obligation or compensation to Customer. Feedback does not include Customer Data, Customer Confidential Information, or any proprietary methodologies, processes, or business strategies disclosed by Customer. Lattice will not disclose the source of any Feedback to third parties without Customer's consent.
accepted_findings.external_comment: The current Feedback clause is broader than necessary for a SaaS provider's legitimate interests. The grant of 'transferable, sublicensable' rights and the language 'or the business of Lattice' extend beyond product improvement and could permit Lattice to commercialize Customer's strategic insights. We propose limiting the Feedback license to product development and excluding Customer's confidential information and proprietary business strategies.
accepted_findings.materiality_rationale: An unrestricted Feedback clause allows Lattice to take Customer's proprietary business processes, strategic suggestions, or competitive insights (e.g., 'you should enter the X market' or 'we use your product in Y novel way') and freely transfer or sublicense them to third parties, including competitors. If Customer shares substantive operational or strategic feedback, the current language effectively converts confidential business intelligence into a free, perpetual license for Lattice to exploit. This chills Customer's willingness to collaborate on product development.
accepted_findings.position: Feedback license limited to incorporation into Subscription Services. No transferable or sublicensable rights. Exclusion for Customer Confidential Information and proprietary methodologies.
accepted_findings.fallback: Feedback license remains broad, but Lattice agrees not to disclose the source of Feedback and to treat any Feedback that constitutes Customer Confidential Information as confidential under Section 7.
accepted_findings.jurisdiction_assumed: Delaware (stated in 10.1). Delaware law would permit enforcement of broad IP assignments via Feedback clauses absent public-policy exceptions.
accepted_findings.suggested_text: If Customer or its Authorized Users provide Lattice with any suggestions, recommendations, ideas, or feedback regarding the Subscription Services ('Feedback'), Customer hereby grants Lattice a non-exclusive, perpetual, irrevocable, worldwide, royalty-free right and license to use, copy, modify, and incorporate such Feedback into the Subscription Services and related products, without any obligation or compensation to Customer. Feedback does not include Customer Data, Customer Confidential Information, or any proprietary methodologies, processes, or business strategies disclosed by Customer. Lattice will not disclose the source of any Feedback to third parties without Customer's consent.
accepted_findings._rationale_explanation: References general feedback clause concerns (competitors, business intelligence) that apply to any B2B SaaS contract, but doesn't engage with this specific contract's structure, leverage position, or dollar value to contextualize the risk or weigh trade-offs.

accepted_findings.proposed_text: 10.10 Publicity. Neither Party may use the other Party's name, logo, trademarks, or identify the other Party as a customer or service provider in any press release, marketing material, case study, website, or other public communication without the other Party's prior written consent, which may be withheld in such Party's sole discretion. Notwithstanding the foregoing, Lattice may include Customer's name and logo in a list of customers on Lattice's website or in sales materials, provided that such use is limited to factual identification and does not imply endorsement.
accepted_findings.external_comment: The Agreement does not address whether Lattice may use Customer's name or logo in marketing materials, case studies, or public client lists. We propose adding a mutual publicity provision that requires consent for substantive use while permitting Lattice to include Customer in a factual client list.
accepted_findings.materiality_rationale: Without a publicity restriction, Lattice may publish case studies, press releases, or detailed descriptions of Customer's use of the Subscription Services without Customer's knowledge or approval. This creates reputational risk if the case study discloses sensitive business information, mischaracterizes the relationship, or associates Customer with Lattice during a period when Customer is dissatisfied with the service. For companies in regulated industries or those concerned with competitive intelligence, uncontrolled publicity can reveal strategic initiatives to competitors or trigger regulatory inquiries.
accepted_findings.position: Mutual consent required for any use of name, logo, or identification in marketing materials. Lattice may include Customer in a factual client list only.
accepted_findings.fallback: Lattice may use Customer's name and logo in a client list and may request consent for case studies, which Customer will not unreasonably withhold. Customer has the right to review and approve any case study or detailed description before publication.
accepted_findings.jurisdiction_assumed: Delaware (stated in 10.1). Delaware recognizes common-law publicity rights and would likely permit reasonable contractual restrictions on use of business names and marks.
accepted_findings.suggested_text: 10.10 Publicity. Neither Party may use the other Party's name, logo, trademarks, or identify the other Party as a customer or service provider in any press release, marketing material, case study, website, or other public communication without the other Party's prior written consent, which may be withheld in such Party's sole discretion. Notwithstanding the foregoing, Lattice may include Customer's name and logo in a list of customers on Lattice's website or in sales materials, provided that such use is limited to factual identification and does not imply endorsement.
accepted_findings._rationale_explanation: References general principles about reputational risk and regulated industries, but doesn't engage with this specific contract's actual publicity provision, the high-leverage position, or whether the mutual consent + client list language already addresses these concerns adequately.

rejected_findings.source_text: All invoices are payable in U.S. dollars and are due net sixty (60) days from the date of invoice.
rejected_findings.proposed_text: All invoices are payable in U.S. dollars and are due net thirty (30) days from the date of invoice.
rejected_findings.external_comment: We'd like to align payment terms with our standard AP cycle at Net-30. This is a meaningful cash-flow consideration on an annual-advance invoicing model.
rejected_findings.materiality_rationale: Net-60 on annual-advance invoicing extends Customer's cash outlay by 30 days relative to Net-30, which is material on a mid-market SaaS subscription; Profile specifies Net-30 as standard, with Net-45 acceptable for critical-path deals only.
rejected_findings.fallback: Net-45 from date of invoice, in exchange for a discount or other commercial concession.
rejected_findings.jurisdiction_assumed: Delaware (contract references Delaware law and Delaware corporation; payment-term standards do not vary materially by jurisdiction in U.S. SaaS contracts)
rejected_findings.suggested_text: All invoices are payable in U.S. dollars and are due net thirty (30) days from the date of invoice.
rejected_findings.rejection_reason: Proposed Net 30 shortens payment timing from Net 60; Customer-side reviews must not decrease Net-term days.
